figures.caption: Figure 5: Selectivity across image–caption, image–text, and image–document settings under unperturbed, marker-removal, and marker-swap conditions in Qwen2.5-VL-32B. Marker perturbations have little effect in image–text, but substantially reduce performance in image– caption and image–document, where models rely heavily on markers.

Figure 15: Selectivity of InternVL3-14B after learned interventions at different layer depths. In early and middle layers, interventions on both marker tokens and content tokens drive selectivity far below zero, indicating strong source-modality misattribution. Marker-token interventions are generally more effective at the shallowest layers, while content-token interventions remain comparably strong through part of the middle layers. Unlike Gemma3-12B, the intervention effect persists over a broader range of depths, but both curves recover in later layers and return to positive selectivity. Dashed and dotted lines show the selectivity of the unintervened model and under the token-level marker-swap condition.

Figure 2: Left: Aggregated source-modality selectivity across VLMs and datasets. Error bars indicate standard deviation across datasets. ⋆ marks VLMs with special image wrapper tokens. Right: averaged rate of valid responses under inconsistent input pairs and under single modality inputs across datasets.

Figure 11: Illustration of the freeze-remove condition. We first collect hidden activations at content-token positions from a normal run with intact markers, then patch these activations into a run where the marker tokens are removed. This tests whether symbolic marker information has been contextualized into the content-token representations.

Figure 8: Selectivity of Qwen2.5-VL-32B after learned interventions at different layer depths. In early and intermediate layers, both intervention types can cause selectivity below zero, indicating that the model retrieves information from the non-queried modality more often than from the queried one. Marker-token interventions are especially effective, pushing selectivity close to −1 across a broad range of layers. Dashed and dotted lines show the selectivity of the unintervened model and under the token-level marker-swap condition.

Figure 12: Selectivity across image–caption, image–text, and image–document settings under unperturbed, marker-removal, and marker-swap conditions in Gemma-3-12B. Marker perturbations have little effect in image–text, but substantially reduce performance in image–caption and image–document, where models rely heavily on markers.

Figure 14: Selectivity of Gemma-3-12B after learned interventions at different layer depths. In early layers, marker-token interventions are more effective than content-token interventions and can drive selectivity below zero, indicating successful source-modality misattribution. Content-token interventions have weaker and less consistent effects, although they still reduce selectivity substantially at shallow layers. In contrast to Qwen, both intervention types lose their effect by the middle layers, after which selectivity returns to ceiling performance, suggesting that the representations causally accessible for reversing modality attribution are concentrated in Gemma-3-12B’s early layers. Dashed and dotted lines show the selectivity of the unintervened model and under the token-level marker-swap condition.

Figure 13: Selectivity across image–caption, image–text, and image–document settings under unperturbed, marker-removal, and marker-swap conditions in InternVL3-14B. Marker perturbations have somewhat effect in image–text, but substantially reduce performance in image–caption and image–document, where models rely heavily on markers.

Figure 3: Performance on the purely symbolic retrieval task across four arbitrary label assignments. Each bar shows selectivity when image and caption content are associated with abstract labels rather than modality names. LabelA–LabelB indicates that LabelA is associated with image content and LabelB with caption content.
Figure 10: Prompt templates used for GPT-based evaluation in the inconsistent, image-only, and text-only settings.
